description: XGBoost classifier that predicts a resume's quality grade (weak / average / strong) from 27 hand-engineered structural and linguistic features.
top_predictive_features: quantification_count (17.1%) — most impactful; quantified achievements strongly predict strong grade
char_count + word_count (23.3% combined) — resume length is a strong proxy for completeness
action_verb_count (8.4%) — verb-driven writing correlates with higher grades
has_education (6.2%) — presence of education section reliably separates weak from average
section_count (6.0%) — structural completeness matters across all classes
zero_importance_note: These boolean flags likely have near-zero variance in the training data (most resumes include them). Consider dropping in future iterations to reduce noise.

training.class_imbalance_handling: inverse-frequency sample weights per training example

evaluation_metrics.status: pending — run training/train_scorer.py and copy results from SCORER_EVALUATION_RESULTS.md

history.note: baseline — first production model; evaluation metrics not yet captured (run train_scorer.py to populate)

usage.load:
import pickle
with open('scorer_model.pkl','rb') as f:
    payload = pickle.load(f)
model = payload['model']
feature_names = payload['feature_names']
usage.predict:
from modules.features import extract_features
feats = extract_features(cv_text, career_label)
import pandas as pd
X = pd.DataFrame([feats])[feature_names]
predicted_label = model.predict(X)[0]   # 0=weak, 1=average, 2=strong
proba = model.predict_proba(X)[0]       # [P(weak), P(average), P(strong)]

known_limitations: 7 features have zero importance — likely due to low variance; consider removing in v2
model is field-agnostic at inference time unless career_label is passed to extract_features
scores are calibrated against rule-based labels, not a held-out human evaluation set
creative class has very few samples; quality predictions for creative resumes may be unreliable
